title: Chapter 7, Verse 13
shlok:
त्रिभिर्गुणमयैर्भावैरेभिः सर्वमिदं जगत् ।
मोहितं नाभिजानाति मामेभ्यः परमव्ययम् ॥
transliteration:
tribhir guṇamayair bhāvair ebhiḥ sarvam idaṁ jagat
mohitaṁ nābhijānāti mām ebhyaḥ param avyayam
translation: Deluded by the three modes of nature — goodness, passion, and ignorance — the entire world fails to recognize Me, who am beyond them and imperishable.
interpretation: Krishna explains here why most people cannot perceive the divine directly. The three gunas — sattva (clarity and goodness), rajas (passion and restless striving), and tamas (inertia and ignorance) — act as a veil over the mind. 'Mohitam' means deluded or bewildered. Because all beings in the material world operate under the pull of these three qualities, they see only the surface play of appearances and miss the underlying, unchanging reality that Krishna calls 'param avyayam' — beyond the modes and imperishable. This verse continues the chapter's exploration of maya, explaining how even sincere seekers get caught in the fabric of the three gunas before they can see clearly.
application: The transition from CSM to PM is not just a title change — it requires a shift in how you see and think, and that shift is often obscured by the very modes this verse describes. Tamas shows up as self-doubt and avoidance: telling yourself you are not ready yet. Rajas shows up as anxious busyness without a clear direction, mistaking motion for progress. Sattva — the quality that allows clear seeing — is available, but it requires stepping back from the noise long enough to recognize the pattern. The verse is a quiet reminder that the thing obscuring the path is not the path itself.
question: Which mode — doubt and avoidance, restless urgency, or something else — most often pulls you away from the work that actually matters to you right now?
